capitalOne.journeySub: Four stages from application to offer. Each stage filters hard — show up ready.

journey.what: Initial screen with a technical recruiter. High-level: background, role interest, salary expectations, timeline. Non-technical.
journey.evaluated: Culture fit at a surface level, motivation, whether your background maps to an open req.

journey.what: Timed CodeSignal screen. Four problems of increasing weight (100 / 200 / 300 / 400). You run tests locally; only the final submit counts.
journey.evaluated: Correctness, pattern recognition, and pacing. Published pass threshold is ~600; aim for 800+ for a safe signal.

journey.what: Four back-to-back rounds (~45 min each) with breaks: Coding, Coding + Job Fit, System Design OR Behavioral, Business Case.
journey.evaluated: Technical depth, communication, structured reasoning, and fit against the 4 Capital One values across every round.

journey.what: Debrief happens same-week. If you pass, recruiter returns with a verbal offer, then a written packet.
journey.evaluated: Nothing technical. At this stage it's compensation negotiation and start-date alignment.

capitalOne.distinctiveSub: Prep that works for FAANG won't cover this. These are the lenses Capital One interviewers use.

distinctive.title: A bank, not a social network
distinctive.body: Capital One regulates itself like a bank because it is one. Every system design must answer: is this PCI-in-scope? What's the audit trail? How do we prove we did the right thing to a regulator? Interviewers probe for this reflex.

distinctive.title: Money demands strong consistency
distinctive.body: You cannot eventually-consistency your way through a ledger. Expect probing on transactions, idempotency, exactly-once processing, and when strong consistency is worth the latency cost. Saying 'Cassandra' for a transfer pipeline is an instant red flag.

distinctive.title: The 4 values are the behavioral spine
distinctive.body: Excellence, Do The Right Thing, Respect for Individuals, Succeed Together. Every behavioral prompt maps to one of these. Rehearsing STAR stories with each value in mind is non-negotiable prep.

distinctive.title: JVM-heavy enterprise stack
distinctive.body: Java is the dominant backend language; AWS is the cloud. Microservices, Kafka for eventing, Postgres/Aurora, observability via Datadog. You won't be tested on brand names, but familiarity with the shape helps you speak their dialect.

capitalOne.planSub: Three tracks. Pick one — come back for the others. The mocks are where real preparation happens.

plan.body: Pull a random code exercise from Capital One's pool. Solve, run, next. Great for warming up before a mock or filling 20-minute slots during the week.

capitalOne.panelIntroBody: Capital One organizes engineering into small cross-functional teams called pods (sometimes squads). A typical pod is ~4 engineers of mixed levels. Power Day panels usually mirror this shape — so in your 4 back-to-back rounds, you're effectively meeting a pod. Exact composition varies team to team; the grid below is the MOST common mix, not a rule.
capitalOne.panelDisclaimer: Composition varies by org and role. Capital One doesn't publish a fixed Power Day panel template — what's below is the pattern reported most often by candidates and backed by the pod-model Capital One uses internally.
capitalOne.panelHeader: The four panelists you're most likely to meet

panel.title: Lead Software Engineer
panel.body: The architectural voice on the panel. Usually runs your system-design round, or jumps in on depth during coding.
panel.level: Lead SWE · 8+ years · sometimes Principal / Senior Lead
panel.probes: Long-term tradeoffs, scale boundaries, failure modes, where the system breaks at 10× load.
panel.tip: They're not scoring brilliance. They're scoring whether you name the cost of a decision out loud.

panel.title: Senior Associate SWE · coding
panel.body: Pod member who'd review your PRs daily if you joined. Usually drives the first coding round.
panel.probes: Correctness, edge cases, clean reasoning, how you NARRATE while you type.
panel.tip: They've watched 10 candidates code this week. Silent thinking reads as 'I don't know'. Keep narrating.

panel.title: Senior SWE · behavioral / cross-pod
panel.body: Often pulled from a different pod for an outside-perspective check. Runs the behavioral or the values-focused round.
panel.probes: STAR structure, 'I' vs 'we', specific numbers, values-aligned examples (Excellence / DTRT / RFI / ST).
panel.tip: This is the panelist who catches generic answers fastest. Every story needs a named teammate, a number, and a verbatim line you actually said.

panel.title: Associate SWE (often TDP)
panel.body: A Technology Development Program associate — new grad in an 18-month, 2-rotation program. Often observes coding + asks job-fit questions.
panel.probes: Culture fit, how you talk about mentoring, how you'd pair-program, your motivation for Capital One specifically.
panel.tip: The Associate is the panelist you'll actually work WITH. How you treat them in the room is a signal the panel absolutely shares in the debrief.

capitalOne.ladderHeader: The engineering ladder you'll hear named
capitalOne.ladderSub: IC track. The manager track runs in parallel. Titles and YOE bands below are roughly what's reported on Levels.fyi and internal Capital One postings; your mileage varies.

ladder.signal: 0–2 years · TDP entry level · 18-month rotational program, 2 rotations on different teams
2–5 years · primary coding panelist role, heavy pod contributor
8+ years · tech leadership across multiple teams, system-design panelist
IC-heavy senior role, often owns a domain or platform

capitalOne.vocabSub: Capital One engineers drop these terms in normal conversation. Using them (correctly, not as buzzwords) signals you've done your homework and that your background fits.

vocab.why: A small cross-functional team (~4 engineers + PM + sometimes designer). Say 'on my last pod' to describe your team.
Load-bearing term on job postings. Means 'built with quality, risk, and compliance thought through up front'. Using it in sys-design signals you understand regulatory context.
Their analog to chapters / guilds. Example: a Frontend CoP. Mention CoPs if asked about learning + mentorship.
Capital One ran an all-in AWS migration and shut down data centers. They describe themselves as cloud-native. Don't propose on-prem solutions in sys-design.
The narrative that Capital One is a tech company that happens to be a bank. Reference it in 'why Capital One' without parroting it.
